title: HTTP Controller API
text: HTTP Controller API - XIS Architecture / HTTP Controller API HTTP Controller API The HTTP Controller API is a special path for raw HTTP endpoints, static apps, games, and REST-like services. It is powerful, but normal XIS page applications usually do not need it. The native variant is still important: it should be easy to build small native HTTP services when the page framework is intentionally not part of the application. When this API is the right tool Most XIS applications should use pages, frontlets, forms, and actions. The HTTP Controller API is meant for cases where that model would get in the way: REST endpoints, small service APIs, static browser clients, games, probes, health checks, and integration endpoints that are not page interactions. This distinction is important for positioning. The existence of HTTP controllers does not mean that ordinary XIS users should write their own JavaScript application and only use XIS as a backend. It means the framework also has a controlled escape hatch for applications that intentionally do not use the page layer. import one.xis.http.Controller; import one.xis.http.Get; import one.xis.http.PathVariable; import one.xis.http.Post; import one.xis.http.RequestBody; import one.xis.http.ResponseEntity; @Controller class CustomerEndpoint { @Get("/api/customers/{id}") CustomerDto find(@PathVariable("id") long id) { return customers.findDto(id); } @Post("/api/customers") ResponseEntity<CustomerDto> create(@RequestBody CustomerForm form) { CustomerDto customer = customers.create(form); return ResponseEntity.created(customer) .addHeader("Location", "/api/customers/" + customer.id()); } } Runtime choices A pure HTTP controller application can run with XIS Boot and the Netty HTTP runtime. In a Spring application, the servlet container remains the natural HTTP runtime. If a project already uses Spring MVC and Spring security conventions heavily, it is usually better to keep using Spring's controller annotations for that part. The advanced case is a mixed XIS application: server-driven pages for the user interface and a few plain HTTP endpoints for assets, diagnostics, or integration. In that case the HTTP filter chain is the right place to keep static resources and custom endpoints away from the page controller flow when necessary. import one.xis.context.Component; import one.xis.http.FilterChain; import one.xis.http.HttpFilter; import one.xis.http.HttpRequest; import one.xis.http.HttpResponse; @Component class DiagnosticsHeaderFilter implements HttpFilter { @Override public void doFilter(HttpRequest request, HttpResponse response, FilterChain chain) { response.addHeader("X-App", "example"); chain.doFilter(request, response); } } Static clients and public resources Static HTML, JavaScript, and assets in the public resource folder are public web resources. They should be served as files, not interpreted as XIS page templates. This is the pattern used by applications that bring their own browser client while still using the XIS HTTP runtime. The rule keeps ownership clear: templates belong to the XIS page layer, public resources belong to the web server side of the application. A custom filter can short-circuit requests when an application has unusual routing needs. SSE and streaming responses Server-sent events fit the HTTP Controller API better than the page action model. They are useful when the browser needs a long-lived stream for refresh signals, progress updates, or other one-way server notifications. The stream should publish small events and let the normal page/frontlet refresh path load substantial data. Native images Small HTTP services are a strong native-image use case because they can avoid the page runtime and the generated JavaScript bundle entirely. The native build should still be benchmarked in the target environment. Native images are excellent for startup and memory shape, but they are not a promise of higher long-running throughput.

title: Microfrontend Architecture
text:
Microfrontends - XIS Architecture / Microfrontends Microfrontend Architecture XIS fits vertical ownership: a service can own its business logic and the page or frontlet that presents it. The browser can still behave like one coherent application. Vertical slices XIS microfrontends are not primarily a packaging trick. They are a way to keep business ownership vertical: persistence, service code, page controller, template, validation, and tests can live in the same feature or service boundary. The browser receives a coherent UI, while the code remains split along business responsibilities. Think of a product page in a shop. The visible page is one experience, but catalog, price, discount, stock, store-pickup, notification, and cart behavior may belong to different teams and databases. XIS frontlets let those teams contribute focused UI parts without forcing the product page to become a large client-side integration project. The product id belongs to the page URL, not to the frontlet instances. The page and every frontlet can read the same path variable with @PathVariable("id") . Frontlet parameters are still useful, but they should describe the concrete frontlet instance, not repeat context that is already part of the route. ProductPage.html

SKU ${product.sku}

${product.name}

${product.teaser}

ProductPage.java import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; import one.xis.PathVariable; @Page("/products/{id}.html") class ProductPage { private final CatalogService catalogService; ProductPage(CatalogService catalogService) { this.catalogService = catalogService; } @ModelData("product") ProductHeader product(@PathVariable("id") long productId) { return catalogService.header(productId); } } ProductDescriptionFrontlet.html

Description

${description.summary}

  • ${feature}
ProductDescriptionFrontlet.java import one.xis.Frontlet; import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.PathVariable; @Frontlet class ProductDescriptionFrontlet { private final CatalogService catalogService; ProductDescriptionFrontlet(CatalogService catalogService) { this.catalogService = catalogService; } @ModelData("description") ProductDescription description(@PathVariable("id") long productId) { return catalogService.description(productId); } } DeliveryAndDiscountFrontlet.html

Delivery and offers

Delivery: ${offer.deliveryLabel}

Discount: ${offer.discountLabel}

DeliveryAndDiscountFrontlet.java import one.xis.Frontlet; import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.PathVariable; @Frontlet class DeliveryAndDiscountFrontlet { private final PricingService pricingService; DeliveryAndDiscountFrontlet(PricingService pricingService) { this.pricingService = pricingService; } @ModelData("offer") ProductOffer offer(@PathVariable("id") long productId) { return pricingService.currentOffer(productId); } } StockAndPickupFrontlet.html

Availability

${stock.availableText}

Currently unavailable online.

StockAndPickupFrontlet.java import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.FormData; import one.xis.Frontlet; import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.PathVariable; @Frontlet class StockAndPickupFrontlet { private final InventoryService inventoryService; StockAndPickupFrontlet(InventoryService inventoryService) { this.inventoryService = inventoryService; } @ModelData("stock") StockStatus stock(@PathVariable("id") long productId) { return inventoryService.status(productId); } @FormData("notification") NotificationForm notification() { return new NotificationForm(); } @Action void notifyWhenAvailable(@PathVariable("id") long productId, @FormData("notification") NotificationForm form) { inventoryService.registerNotification(productId, form.email()); } } AddToCartFrontlet.html
AddToCartFrontlet.java import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.FormData; import one.xis.Frontlet; import one.xis.PathVariable; @Frontlet class AddToCartFrontlet { private final CartService cartService; AddToCartFrontlet(CartService cartService) { this.cartService = cartService; } @FormData("cartLine") CartLineForm cartLine() { return new CartLineForm(1); } @Action void addToCart(@PathVariable("id") long productId, @FormData("cartLine") CartLineForm form) { cartService.add(productId, form.quantity()); } } The catalog, pricing, inventory, and checkout teams can each own one vertical slice: their persistence, services, controller, template, validation, and tests. The product page stays readable because the route supplies the common product context, while each frontlet decides what it needs to render and refresh. Pages and frontlets across modules A page can be owned by one module and composed with frontlets from another. This is useful for dashboards, shell applications, and distributed business tools. The important rule is that each fragment should still have a clear owner and a clear refresh contract. xis.distributed.hosts=https://catalog.example.com,https://inventory.example.com,https://checkout.example.com xis.distributed.allowed-origins=https://shop.example.com Routing model Distributed applications need predictable routing. URLs should stay meaningful and should not depend on fragile client-side state. XIS can navigate by URL, by page response, and by frontlet refresh, so the architecture can use browser-visible routes where they help and server-side interactions where they keep the workflow simpler. SSO Authentication is the place where distributed applications become visible to users. XIS security and SSO support belong together with the microfrontend story, but the detailed security model lives in the security chapter. The architectural point is simple: separate services should not force separate logins for one logical application. When not to split A small application does not become better just because every screen is a separate artifact. Split when the team structure, deployment model, or business boundary needs it. Otherwise, a single XIS application with well-kept feature packages is often easier to understand.

title: Actions and navigation
text: Actions And Navigation - XIS Core Model / Actions and navigation Actions and navigation XIS actions let a browser event call Java code without turning the page into a handwritten frontend application. The method result describes what should happen next: update the current view, navigate to another page, open a modal, refresh a frontlet, or do nothing visible. Actions are part of the page contract A page controller is not just a URL handler. It is the server-side model of the interactive page. Links, buttons, forms, drag-and-drop targets, and modal commands can all bind to controller methods. This keeps the decision about what is allowed, what is validated, and which model is returned on the server side. The browser still behaves like an SPA. XIS receives the action response, updates only the affected DOM regions, and keeps browser state such as focused elements and client-side widgets as stable as possible. Return types XIS deliberately makes navigation explicit. A controller method can return page data for the current page, a page response, a page URL response, a frontlet response, a modal response, a redirect-like response, or no result when the action is purely server-side. The exact type tells the framework how much of the browser state must change. This is also why tests remain small: a test can call the same page interaction a user would trigger and assert the response type or rendered result without spinning up a separate JavaScript application model. import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.FrontletResponse; import one.xis.ModalResponse; import one.xis.PageResponse; class CartPage { @Action("increase") void increaseQuantity(long lineId) { cart.increase(lineId); // Stay on the current page and refresh the affected model. } @Action("checkout") PageResponse checkout() { return new PageResponse(CheckoutPage.class); } @Action("showShipping") ModalResponse showShippingOptions() { return ModalResponse.open(ShippingModal.class); } @Action("refreshSummary") FrontletResponse refreshSummary() { return new FrontletResponse(CartSummaryFrontlet.class) .targetContainer("cart-summary"); } } Deep links and path variables Pages can expose stable URLs with path variables. This matters for bookmarks, shared links, and browser reloads. The user can enter through a URL, but after that the same page can continue through XIS actions and partial updates. import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; import one.xis.PathVariable; @Page("/products/{id}.html") class ProductPage { @ModelData("product") Product product(@PathVariable("id") long id) { return catalog.findProduct(id); } } Modals and small edit flows Many business applications need small forms that open above an existing list or detail page. In XIS these modals should be treated as normal server-backed form flows: open the modal through an action, validate on the server, close or refresh the relevant part of the page after success. Routers Routers are useful when an application needs explicit routing logic before a page is selected. They should not be used as a replacement for ordinary page controllers. If a URL maps naturally to one page, the page controller is usually the simpler model.

title: Forms and validation
text: Forms And Validation - XIS Core / Forms and validation Forms and validation XIS keeps validation rules on the server to avoid parallel client/server rule sets. The cost is an extra request when validation runs; the gain is one authoritative validation model. Forms can cover normal input fields, nested objects, lists, type conversion, custom validators, validation messages, action validation failures, file uploads, and modal dialogs. The same binding model is used throughout. Why server-side validation is the default Client-side validation can feel immediate, but it often creates two rule systems: one in the browser and one on the server. Those systems drift. XIS favors one source of truth and then makes validation feedback part of the normal page update flow. This does not prevent small browser-side conveniences. It only means correctness belongs to the server. The framework transports validation errors back to the UI and lets templates render them consistently. A form is a Java contract plus HTML binding The controller exposes a form object with @FormData . The template binds normal HTML controls to that object. Validation annotations stay on the Java type, so tests and runtime behavior use the same rules. import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.FormData; import one.xis.Page; import one.xis.validation.EMail; import one.xis.validation.MinLength; @Page("/contact.html") class ContactPage { @FormData("contact") ContactForm contact() { return new ContactForm("", "", ""); } @Action("send") void send(@FormData("contact") ContactForm form) { // Called only after binding and validation have succeeded. contactService.send(form.name(), form.email(), form.message()); } record ContactForm( @MinLength(1) String name, @EMail String email, @MinLength(10) String message) { } } <form xis:binding="contact"> <label>Name<input xis:binding="name"></label> <label>Email<input xis:binding="email"></label> <label>Message<textarea xis:binding="message"></textarea></label> <button type="submit" xis:action="send">Send</button> </form> Modal forms are ordinary forms Small dialog forms are common in business applications: edit one field, confirm a change, add a row, or collect a short decision without leaving the current page. XIS modals use the same controller, template, model data, form data, action, and validation concepts as normal pages.

title: Pages and templates
text: Pages And Templates - XIS Core / Pages and templates Pages and templates XIS pages are defined by a backend controller and an HTML template. Model methods provide data, actions handle user interaction, and the browser runtime applies the resulting changes without turning the application into a handwritten frontend state machine. The page as a vertical slice A page controller describes what the page can do. The HTML template describes how that page is shown. XIS connects both sides: it invokes model methods, binds action calls, transports form data, and updates the browser after the server has produced a response. The browser does not become the owner of routing or application state. It executes the XIS runtime, keeps the DOM in sync, and avoids browser full page reloads. Even a transition from one XIS page to another is not a classic browser reload: the XIS JavaScript runtime receives the server-rendered result and replaces the relevant DOM elements. That is why the programming model can feel like request/response code even though the browser behaves like an SPA. Template syntax belongs to the model XIS templates are plain HTML plus XIS attributes and tags. Conditions, iteration, navigation attributes, forms, validation messages, dynamic classes, raw content, includes, and frontlet declarations are documented as part of the core UI model because they are the public surface the developer writes every day. Variable scope A template is evaluated in the scope of the current component. A page has its page model. A frontlet has its frontlet model. An include is initialized inside the surrounding page or frontlet and therefore sees the surrounding variables. Nested frontlets can also read parameters from their containing frontlet. A directly supplied frontlet parameter wins over an inherited parameter with the same name. Use this for values that really belong to a concrete frontlet instance. Shared route context, such as a product id in /products/{id}.html , should normally stay a path variable and be read with @PathVariable . <main xis:frontlet-container="buy-box" xis:default-frontlet="AddToCartFrontlet?layout=compact"> </main> In this example the frontlet receives layout as a frontlet parameter because it describes how this specific buy box should render. The product id can still come from the current page URL: @Frontlet class AddToCartFrontlet { @ModelData("cartLine") CartLineForm cartLine(@PathVariable("id") long productId, @FrontletParameter("layout") String layout) { return cartService.newLine(productId, layout); } }

title: Request lifecycle
text:
Request Lifecycle - XIS Core / Request lifecycle Request lifecycle XIS turns page loads, navigation, actions, forms, shared values, client state, and refresh events into one coherent browser/server flow. Lifecycle picture 1. Browser event Link, button, form, modal, drag/drop, or refresh signal. 2. Small XIS request The runtime sends the action, parameters, form data, and client id. 3. Controller method Java code validates, calls services, and returns a response. 4. Server-rendered result XIS renders the affected page, frontlet, modal, or model fragment. 5. DOM replacement The browser runtime replaces the relevant elements; the shell stays alive. Initial load On the first request, XIS resolves the page, creates the model data, renders the HTML, and sends the page together with the runtime scripts needed for later interactions. A reload or deep link must be able to reconstruct the page without relying on hidden client state. Action request A click, form submit, drag-and-drop action, or modal command sends a small request to the server. The controller method runs, validation is applied where relevant, and the response describes the next UI state. The browser does not reimplement that decision. It applies the server response. import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/counter.html") class CounterPage { private final CounterService counterService; CounterPage(CounterService counterService) { this.counterService = counterService; } @ModelData("count") int count() { return counterService.currentValue(); } @Action("increase") void increase() { counterService.increase(); } }
${count}
Partial updates XIS can update a page region, a frontlet, a modal, or the whole page. The framework chooses the concrete DOM work from the response and the template structure. This is where XIS produces SPA behavior without asking the application developer to maintain a separate JavaScript state tree. The phrase "whole page" still does not mean a browser full page reload. It means that the XIS runtime replaces the page content in the live browser document. The JavaScript runtime stays loaded, the application shell remains alive, and the transition is handled as a controlled DOM update. Client state Some browser state is inherently local: focus, input composition, scroll positions, and custom JavaScript widgets. XIS should preserve it when possible, but it should not become the source of business truth. Durable state belongs in the server model or in explicit browser storage fields. Refresh events Server-triggered refresh events invalidate page or frontlet data. They do not replace the lifecycle. After an event, the browser asks for the affected model through the usual XIS path and then applies the resulting update.

title: Runtime model
text: Runtime Model - XIS Core / Runtime model Runtime model XIS separates the programming model from the runtime integration. Most application code should depend on one runtime and then use the same controller annotations and templates. Runtime choices XIS Boot The standalone runtime for lightweight XIS applications. Lightweight means a smaller infrastructure surface, not a smaller ambition. Classic web applications with templates, validation, authentication, SQL, and jobs are in scope. XIS Spring The right choice when the application is already a Spring Boot application or when the Spring ecosystem already provides important infrastructure the project relies on. XIS Boot Native The native-image path for standalone applications that should deploy as small native executables or compact containers. HTTP Controller API An advanced path for raw HTTP endpoints, special static apps, games, and native REST-like services. Normal XIS page applications usually do not need it. Choosing between XIS Boot and Spring If an application already lives in Spring Boot, staying with Spring is usually the right choice. The same is true when the application depends heavily on Spring ecosystem infrastructure that would otherwise have to be recreated as XIS components. XIS Boot is the smaller standalone path. It is especially attractive for microfrontend-oriented applications and services where the web UI should stay close to the feature code, while the runtime should not bring a large general-purpose application platform with it. A small amount of ordinary infrastructure code is not a reason to abandon XIS Boot; the question is whether the project genuinely needs the breadth of the Spring ecosystem. XIS Boot also has onboard support for the cloud-native path: the same standalone programming model can be built as a runnable JVM jar or, with XIS Boot Native, as a GraalVM native executable with generated metadata for components, resources, proxies, and reflection. That native path is not an add-on architecture; it is part of the reason to choose XIS Boot for deployable services with a deliberately small runtime and infrastructure surface. If cloud-native deployment is a primary requirement and the application does not already need Spring, XIS Boot is usually the stronger recommendation. Spring can be used, but the native-image path tends to be heavier and more sensitive to the surrounding ecosystem. XIS Boot keeps the runtime surface smaller and the generated native metadata closer to the framework features the application actually uses. Dependency shape Normal applications choose one runtime dependency. That runtime brings the controller and validation APIs transitively. Lower-level modules are documented because they are public, but most applications should not assemble their own runtime from individual internal pieces. plugins { id "java" id "one.xis.plugin" version "0.18.0" } dependencies { implementation "one.xis:xis-boot" // or: implementation "one.xis:xis-spring" // or: implementation "one.xis:xis-boot-native" } import one.xis.boot.XISBootApplication; import one.xis.boot.XISBootRunner; @XISBootApplication public class Application { public static void main(String[] args) { XISBootRunner.run(Application.class, args); } } Static resources Public files live under src/main/resources/public and are served without the public path segment. XIS also uses public CSS and JavaScript resources when building the root page. Concrete public files can be served directly; the root path itself is not treated as a public directory.

title: MongoDB
text: MongoDB - XIS Data / MongoDB MongoDB MongoDB support gets its own chapter: dependencies, configuration, document mapping, repositories, JSON queries, and change streams. Documents MongoDB works best when the aggregate shape already fits the document model. XIS documents mapping, identifiers, repository operations, and the difference between document updates and relational joins without pretending that MongoDB is just SQL with another syntax. import one.xis.mongodb.MongoDocument; import one.xis.mongodb.MongoField; import one.xis.mongodb.MongoId; @MongoDocument("customers") class Customer { @MongoId String customerId; @MongoField("first_name") String firstName; String lastName; } Repositories Repository methods should make persistence explicit. Loading a document and changing a Java object should not silently write data back. This keeps the mental model aligned with services, DTOs, and server-driven UI actions. import one.xis.mongodb.MongoCrudRepository; import one.xis.mongodb.MongoQuery; import one.xis.mongodb.MongoRepository; import java.util.List; @MongoRepository interface CustomerRepository extends MongoCrudRepository<Customer, String> { @MongoQuery("{ lastName: ?0 }") List<Customer> findByLastName(String lastName); } Change streams MongoDB change streams can pair well with XIS refresh events. A change in the database can invalidate a page or frontlet, and the browser can then reload the affected model through the normal XIS lifecycle. import one.xis.RefreshEvent; import one.xis.RefreshEventPublisher; import one.xis.context.Component; import one.xis.mongodb.MongoChangeEvent; import one.xis.mongodb.MongoWatch; @Component class CustomerChanges { private final RefreshEventPublisher refreshEvents; CustomerChanges(RefreshEventPublisher refreshEvents) { this.refreshEvents = refreshEvents; } @MongoWatch(Customer.class) void customerChanged(MongoChangeEvent<Customer> event) { refreshEvents.publish(new RefreshEvent("customers")); } } Current maturity MongoDB is part of the XIS documentation because the framework has support and tests for it. The main production path has historically been SQL, so treat MongoDB integration like any newer persistence path: keep application tests close to the repositories and change-stream behavior you rely on.

title: SQL repositories
text: SQL Repositories - XIS Data / SQL repositories SQL repositories XIS SQL is explicit. Reading a row creates an object, but changing that object never changes the database by itself. Writes happen through repository operations. This is a deliberate contrast to JPA-style persistence contexts. XIS favors explicit repository calls and a decoupled service shape. If an application requires an object-level transaction monitor and automatic dirty checking, JPA may be the better persistence model. No hidden persistence context XIS SQL maps rows to objects and objects to repository operations. It does not keep loaded entities in a session and it does not inspect changed objects at the end of a transaction. A database write happens when code calls an insert, update, save, delete, or explicitly annotated repository method. That style is deliberately simple. It fits services and microfrontend applications where the boundary between reading data, changing data, and publishing UI updates should remain visible in the code. import one.xis.sql.CrudRepository; import one.xis.sql.Entity; import one.xis.sql.Repository; import one.xis.sql.Save; import one.xis.sql.Select; import java.util.List; @Entity("customers") record Customer(Long id, String firstName, String lastName) { } @Repository interface CustomerRepository extends CrudRepository<Customer, Long> { @Select("select * from customers where last_name = {lastName}") List<Customer> findByLastName(String lastName); @Save Customer save(Customer customer); } Customer customer = customerRepository.findById(42L).orElseThrow(); customer = new Customer(customer.id(), "Ada", customer.lastName()); // Nothing has been written yet. The write is explicit: customerRepository.save(customer); Schema changes are part of the application The DDL builder and change-set model give XIS applications a small migration mechanism without turning SQL into an ORM. Change sets are explicit, ordered, and recorded in the database so the application can evolve its schema in a controlled way. import one.xis.ddl.Change; import one.xis.ddl.ChangeSet; import one.xis.ddl.DDL; @ChangeSet("customer-schema") class CustomerSchema { @Change("001-create-customers") void createCustomers(DDL ddl) { var customers = ddl.createTableIfNotExists("customers"); customers.addColumn("id").bigint().generatedIdentity().primaryKey(); customers.addColumn("name").varchar(200).notNull(); customers.addColumn("email").varchar(200).notNull(); } } XIS discovers change sets at startup when xis-sql and a DataSource are present. The full SQL guide explains DDL builder and change sets , including ordering, the migration history table, and supported schema operations. Full SQL surface The full SQL guide covers DataSource configuration, optional HikariCP, entity mapping, relations, JSON columns, repositories, select, insert, update, save, delete, DDL change sets, explicit transactions, functions, and stored procedures.

title: First application
text:
First Application - XIS Getting Started / First application First application The first XIS page should show the complete rhythm: controller, template, run command, and a test. Testing belongs here, not only in a late reference chapter. Minimal page controller package example; import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; import one.xis.WelcomePage; @WelcomePage @Page("/hello.html") class HelloPage { private int counter; @ModelData String greeting() { return "Hello XIS"; } @ModelData int count() { return counter; } @Action void increment() { counter++; } } Matching template

${greeting}

Count: ${count}

Add the test immediately XIS should feel TDD-friendly from the first page. Build a page, assert the rendered state, click the action, and assert the updated state. The examples in this documentation are intended to come from executable sample projects, so the code shown here stays tied to something that actually compiles and runs. Treat documentation examples as sample code, not as decoration. The closer they stay to executable projects, the less likely the documentation is to drift away from the framework. Continue with testing from the start

title: Quickstart
text:
Quickstart - XIS Getting Started / Quickstart Quickstart Build a small XIS application, run it, validate it, and add the first test. This is the shortest practical path from an empty project to a working page. This quickstart creates a small XIS application with Spring Boot. The standalone XIS Boot runtime follows the same page, template, and action model; only the application entry point and dependencies differ. Prerequisites Java 17 or newer Gradle 8 or newer Java is the shortest path through this quickstart. XIS also supports Groovy 4+ and Kotlin controllers and form DTOs; see Groovy support and Kotlin support when you want to write the server-side code in another JVM language. Gradle Setup settings.gradle rootProject.name = "my-xis-app" build.gradle plugins { id "java" id "org.springframework.boot" version "3.3.0" id "io.spring.dependency-management" version "1.1.5" id "one.xis.plugin" version "0.18.0" } group = "example" version = "0.0.1-SNAPSHOT" repositories { mavenCentral() } dependencies { implementation "one.xis:xis-spring" } tasks.withType(JavaCompile) { options.encoding = "UTF-8" } test { useJUnitPlatform() } The one.xis.plugin plugin adds the XIS annotation processor, copies HTML templates from src/main/java into the runtime resources, configures the XIS test starter, adds the XIS scaffolding tasks, aligns XIS dependency versions to the plugin version, and adds XIS Boot run/build tasks when you use xis-boot . You do not add xis-test or xis-boot-starter-test yourself in this setup; the plugin adds the matching test dependency automatically. See Gradle plugin and tools for the complete task overview. Application Class src/main/java/example/Application.java package example; import org.springframework.boot.SpringApplication; import org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.SpringBootApplication; @SpringBootApplication class Application { public static void main(String[] args) { SpringApplication.run(Application.class, args); } } Where Templates Go XIS templates normally live next to the Java controller in the same package. If the controller is: src/main/java/example/dashboard/DashboardPage.java then the matching template is: src/main/java/example/dashboard/DashboardPage.html This is intentional. You usually work on the controller and template together, and keeping them side by side makes that relationship visible in the project tree. The XIS Gradle plugin copies these HTML files into the application resources during the build. The plugin can generate missing templates for page and frontlet controllers: ./gradlew xisTemplates Run this after adding a controller when you want XIS to create the template file in the right package. It is often worth writing the controller methods first, even if their bodies are still simple. When xisTemplates sees @ModelData , @FormData , and @Action methods, it can generate expressions, repeated blocks, forms, validation message placeholders, and action buttons that already match the controller. Generated templates are starting points; edit them like normal HTML. The plugin can also generate starter integration tests for page controllers: ./gradlew xisTests Generated tests use @XisBootTest , register the page controller in the XIS test context, and open the page through its URL. The required XIS test starter is added automatically by the plugin. Generated tests compile before the full page behavior is implemented, so you can use them for a TDD-style workflow: sketch the page class and its model/form/action methods, run ./gradlew xisTemplates xisTests , edit the generated test until it describes the UI behavior you want, then implement the services and refine the template until the test passes. When the first version of a page is ready, run XIS validation checks manually: ./gradlew xisValidate This is also the command you usually want in a CI pipeline. It stops at the first validation problem by default and points to the affected template file and line. First Page The first page may feel familiar if you remember simpler request-oriented web models: a controller exposes data and a template renders it. XIS keeps that simple programming shape, but it is not classic server-side rendering. The browser runs a SPA-style DOM application; XIS hides the client-server protocol, DOM replacement, navigation, and refresh logic under the hood. src/main/java/example/dashboard/DashboardPage.java package example.dashboard; import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; import one.xis.WelcomePage; @WelcomePage @Page("/index.html") class DashboardPage { @ModelData String title() { return "Dashboard"; } } Create the template manually or run ./gradlew xisTemplates and refine the generated content. Because the controller already has a title model method, the generated template contains a ${title} expression as a useful starting point: src/main/java/example/dashboard/DashboardPage.html ${title}

${title}

Your first XIS page is running.

Run the app: ./gradlew bootRun Open: http://localhost:8080/ @WelcomePage marks the default entry page. The page is also available directly at /index.html . To generate a first test skeleton for the page, run: ./gradlew xisTests This creates src/test/java/example/dashboard/DashboardPageTest.java if the file does not already exist. The generated test is intentionally small: it uses @XisBootTest , registers the page controller in the test context, opens /index.html , and leaves space for assertions against the rendered document. Add an Action src/main/java/example/dashboard/CounterPage.java package example.dashboard; import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/counter.html") class CounterPage { private int count; @ModelData int count() { return count; } @Action void increment() { count++; } } src/main/java/example/dashboard/CounterPage.html Counter

Counter

Current count: ${count}

The action is invoked through XIS. You do not create a REST endpoint for it. You can generate the test file with ./gradlew xisTests ; you do not have to create the boilerplate yourself. The generated file is intentionally small, so make the behavior explicit by adding the click and assertions: src/test/java/example/dashboard/CounterPageTest.java package example.dashboard; import one.xis.boot.test.XisBootTest; import one.xis.context.IntegrationTestContext; import one.xis.test.InTestContext; import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test; import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertEquals; @XisBootTest class CounterPageTest { private IntegrationTestContext context; @InTestContext private CounterPage counterPage; @Test void incrementUpdatesCounter() { var client = context.openPage("/counter.html"); var document = client.getDocument(); assertEquals("0", document.getElementById("count").getInnerText()); document.getElementById("increment").click(); assertEquals("1", document.getElementById("count").getInnerText()); } } Add a Form With Validation Forms bind HTML controls to a Java object. XIS deserializes the submitted values, validates annotations, and calls the action only when validation succeeds. src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerForm.java package example.customer; import one.xis.validation.EMail; import one.xis.validation.LabelKey; import one.xis.validation.Mandatory; record CustomerForm(@Mandatory @LabelKey("customer.name") String name, @Mandatory @EMail @LabelKey("customer.email") String email) { } src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerNewPage.java package example.customer; import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.FormData; import one.xis.HtmlFile; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/customers/new.html") @HtmlFile("CustomerFormPage.html") class CustomerNewPage { @FormData("customer") CustomerForm customer() { return new CustomerForm("", ""); } @Action void save(@FormData("customer") CustomerForm customer) { // Store the customer in your service or repository. } } src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerFormPage.html Customer

Customer

src/main/resources/messages.properties customer.name=Name customer.email=Email validation.mandatory=${label} is required validation.email=Please enter a valid email address xis:message-for prints the field message. prints form-level validation messages. xis:error-class and xis:error-style let CSS or inline prototype styling highlight fields and labels while an error exists. Add SQL Update the dependencies: build.gradle dependencies { implementation "one.xis:xis-spring" implementation "one.xis:xis-sql" runtimeOnly "org.postgresql:postgresql:42.7.11" } Create a local PostgreSQL database: createdb xis_quickstart Point the application at that database: src/main/resources/application.properties xis.sql.url=jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/xis_quickstart xis.sql.driver-class-name=org.postgresql.Driver If your local PostgreSQL needs explicit credentials, also add xis.sql.user and xis.sql.password . Add a DDL change set for the customer table. XIS discovers change-set classes at startup, runs missing changes against the configured DataSource , and records them in __xis_schema_change , so the table is created without a separate SQL script: src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerSchema.java package example.customer; import one.xis.ddl.Change; import one.xis.ddl.ChangeSet; import one.xis.ddl.DDL; @ChangeSet("customer-schema") class CustomerSchema { @Change("001-create-customers") void createCustomers(DDL ddl) { var customers = ddl.createTableIfNotExists("customers"); customers.addColumn("id").bigint().generatedIdentity().primaryKey(); customers.addColumn("name").varchar(200).notNull(); customers.addColumn("email").varchar(200).notNull(); } } Add the SQL entity, repository, and service: src/main/java/example/customer/Customer.java package example.customer; import one.xis.sql.Entity; @Entity("customers") class Customer { Long id; String name; String email; } src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerRepository.java package example.customer; import one.xis.sql.CrudRepository; import one.xis.sql.Repository; @Repository interface CustomerRepository extends CrudRepository { } src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerService.java package example.customer; import java.util.List; interface CustomerService { List findAll(); CustomerForm newForm(); CustomerForm editForm(long id); void save(Long id, CustomerForm form); } src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerServiceImpl.java package example.customer; import one.xis.context.Component; import java.util.List; @Component class CustomerServiceImpl implements CustomerService { private final CustomerRepository customers; CustomerServiceImpl(CustomerRepository customers) { this.customers = customers; } @Override public List findAll() { return customers.findAll(); } @Override public CustomerForm newForm() { return new CustomerForm("", ""); } @Override public CustomerForm editForm(long id) { var customer = customers.findById(id).orElseThrow(); return new CustomerForm(customer.name, customer.email); } @Override public void save(Long id, CustomerForm form) { var customer = new Customer(); customer.id = id; customer.name = form.name(); customer.email = form.email(); customers.save(customer); } } Now let the form page save through the service and navigate back to the customer list: src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerNewPage.java package example.customer; import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.FormData; import one.xis.HtmlFile; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/customers/new.html") @HtmlFile("CustomerFormPage.html") class CustomerNewPage { private final CustomerService customerService; CustomerNewPage(CustomerService customerService) { this.customerService = customerService; } @FormData("customer") CustomerForm customer() { return customerService.newForm(); } @Action Class save(@FormData("customer") CustomerForm customer) { customerService.save(null, customer); return CustomerListPage.class; } } Add A Customer List Add a list page and link it to the form page. src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerListPage.java package example.customer; import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; import java.util.List; @Page("/customers.html") class CustomerListPage { private final CustomerService customerService; CustomerListPage(CustomerService customerService) { this.customerService = customerService; } @ModelData List customers() { return customerService.findAll(); } } src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerListPage.html Customers

Customers

Add customer

Name Email
${customer.name} ${customer.email} Edit

No customers yet.

Add Edit Use a second page controller for editing, but keep the same CustomerFormPage.html template. The page URL carries the customer id as a path variable. src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerEditPage.java package example.customer; import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.FormData; import one.xis.HtmlFile; import one.xis.Page; import one.xis.PathVariable; @Page("/customers/{id}/edit.html") @HtmlFile("CustomerFormPage.html") class CustomerEditPage { private final CustomerService customerService; CustomerEditPage(CustomerService customerService) { this.customerService = customerService; } @FormData("customer") CustomerForm customer(@PathVariable("id") long id) { return customerService.editForm(id); } @Action Class save(@PathVariable("id") long id, @FormData("customer") CustomerForm customer) { customerService.save(id, customer); return CustomerListPage.class; } } The HTML template does not change. The existing customer id comes from the page URL, not from a hidden form field. Move The Customer UI Into Frontlets The customer pages now work end to end: list, new, edit, validation, and SQL persistence. When a part of the UI should be modular or replaceable on its own, move it into frontlets. A frontlet has its own controller and template fragment, and XIS can reload or replace just that fragment instead of rebuilding the whole page. src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerFrontletPage.java package example.customer; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/customer-frontlet.html") class CustomerFrontletPage { } src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerFrontletPage.html Customers

Customers

src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerListFrontlet.java package example.customer; import one.xis.Frontlet; import one.xis.ModelData; import java.util.List; @Frontlet(containerId = "customer-main") class CustomerListFrontlet { private final CustomerService customerService; CustomerListFrontlet(CustomerService customerService) { this.customerService = customerService; } @ModelData List customers() { return customerService.findAll(); } } src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerListFrontlet.html

Name Email
${customer.name} ${customer.email} Edit

No customers yet.

 src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerFormFrontlet.java package example.customer; import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.FormData; import one.xis.Frontlet; import one.xis.FrontletParameter; import one.xis.NullAllowed; @Frontlet(containerId = "customer-main") class CustomerFormFrontlet { private final CustomerService customerService; CustomerFormFrontlet(CustomerService customerService) { this.customerService = customerService; } @FormData("customer") CustomerForm customer(@FrontletParameter("customerId") @NullAllowed Long customerId) { return customerId == null ? customerService.newForm() : customerService.editForm(customerId); } @Action Class save(@FrontletParameter("customerId") @NullAllowed Long customerId, @FormData("customer") CustomerForm customer) { customerService.save(customerId, customer); return CustomerListFrontlet.class; } } src/main/java/example/customer/CustomerFormFrontlet.html
After a successful save, the action returns CustomerListFrontlet.class , so the list replaces the form in the same container. If validation fails, the action is not called and the form stays visible with validation messages. Frontlets can read @PathVariable values from the current page URL, even when the frontlet itself has no URL. Use @FrontletParameter for values that belong to a specific frontlet instance, such as the customerId passed from the list to the form above. The frontlet action can read the same parameter, so the edit id does not need a hidden form field. Frontlets also become useful when an application grows beyond one deployment unit. Pages and frontlets can be served by different XIS applications, so teams can split a shell page and selected UI fragments across runtimes. That is an advanced setup; continue with Microfrontend Architecture when you need it. Add Authentication Add authentication when the customer screen should require a login: dependencies { implementation "one.xis:xis-spring" implementation "one.xis:xis-sql" implementation "one.xis:xis-authentication" implementation "one.xis:xis-local-credentials" implementation "one.xis:xis-local-credentials-sql" runtimeOnly "org.postgresql:postgresql:42.7.11" } Provide a UserAccountService for the account and roles. xis-local-credentials validates the password separately and xis-local-credentials-sql stores the password hash in SQL: package example.security; import one.xis.auth.UserAccount; import one.xis.auth.UserAccountImpl; import one.xis.auth.UserAccountService; import one.xis.context.Component; import java.util.Optional; import java.util.Set; @Component class DemoUserAccountService implements UserAccountService { @Override public Optional getUserAccount(String userId) { if (!"admin".equals(userId)) { return Optional.empty(); } var user = new UserAccountImpl(); user.setUserId("admin"); user.setRoles(Set.of("ADMIN")); return Optional.of(user); } @Override public void saveUserAccount(UserAccount userAccount) { // Store account profile data here if the application needs it. } } Protect the customer page with @Authenticated when a login is enough: package example.customer; import one.xis.Page; import one.xis.Authenticated; @Authenticated @Page("/customer-frontlet.html") class CustomerFrontletPage { } Use @Roles when named roles are required. For example, keep the list visible to every logged-in user but restrict saving to admins: package example.customer; import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.FormData; import one.xis.Frontlet; import one.xis.FrontletParameter; import one.xis.NullAllowed; import one.xis.Roles; @Frontlet(containerId = "customer-main") class CustomerFormFrontlet { private final CustomerService customerService; CustomerFormFrontlet(CustomerService customerService) { this.customerService = customerService; } @FormData("customer") CustomerForm customer(@FrontletParameter("customerId") @NullAllowed Long customerId) { return customerId == null ? customerService.newForm() : customerService.editForm(customerId); } @Action @Roles("ADMIN") Class save(@FrontletParameter("customerId") @NullAllowed Long customerId, @FormData("customer") CustomerForm customer) { customerService.save(customerId, customer); return CustomerListFrontlet.class; } } The list template can also hide controls that do not match the current user's roles: isUserInRole(...) and isUserInRoles(...) run in the browser. They are useful for a cleaner UI, but they are not security. Keep @Roles or @Authenticated on the Java controller or action that must be protected.

title: Testing from the start
section: Getting Started / Testing from the start
text: Testing From The Start - XIS Getting Started / Testing from the start Testing from the start XIS should make test-driven development feel natural. A controller and template form a small slice that can be rendered, interacted with, and verified without constructing a separate frontend test universe. The early testing promise The first tutorial includes a test before the reader has learned the entire framework. This makes the product story clear: XIS does not hide behavior behind a browser-only client application that is hard to exercise from JVM tests. The important point is not only that XIS has tests. The point is that the ordinary programming model is testable: open a XIS page, inspect the rendered document, click an action, submit a form, and assert the resulting DOM or service state. You do not need to build a parallel REST client and a parallel frontend state model just to test a page workflow. Generated starter tests If the project uses the XIS Gradle plugin, xisTests can generate starter tests for page controllers. This is useful at the beginning of a page because it creates the test file in the right package, wires the XIS test context, and opens the page URL from the page annotation. ./gradlew xisTests A practical workflow is to sketch the controller first, including the @ModelData , @FormData , and @Action methods that define the page contract, then generate templates and tests: ./gradlew xisTemplates xisTests Generated tests are starting points. They should be edited immediately until they describe the behavior you want. The generator avoids boilerplate; it does not replace test design. package example.dashboard; import one.xis.boot.test.XisBootTest; import one.xis.context.IntegrationTestContext; import one.xis.test.InTestContext; import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test; import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertNotNull; @XisBootTest class DashboardPageTest { private IntegrationTestContext context; @InTestContext private DashboardPage dashboardPage; @Test void opensDashboard() { var client = context.openPage("/dashboard.html"); assertNotNull(client.getDocument()); } } Using the annotation without generated tests The generated style is not special to generated files. You can write the same kind of test by hand. Use @XisBootTest when you want the JUnit extension to create the XIS integration-test context and support XIS test annotations such as @InTestContext , @Mock , @Spy , and @Captor . package example.products; import one.xis.boot.test.XisBootTest; import one.xis.context.IntegrationTestContext; import one.xis.test.InTestContext; import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test; import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertEquals; @XisBootTest class ProductPageTest { private IntegrationTestContext context; @InTestContext private ProductService productService; @Test void opensProductPage() { productService.add(new Product(42, "Desk")); var client = context.openPage("/products/42.html"); var document = client.getDocument(); assertEquals("Desk", document.getElementByTagName("h1").getInnerText()); } } @InTestContext registers a real collaborator in the XIS context. The controller sees the same instance through constructor or field injection that the test later inspects. Testing interactions Tests should not stop at "the page opens." XIS tests can interact with the document. A button click can call a server action, a form submit can trigger validation, and the returned DOM can be asserted. @XisBootTest class CounterPageTest { private IntegrationTestContext context; @Test void incrementsCounter() { var client = context.openPage("/counter.html"); var document = client.getDocument(); assertEquals("0", document.getElementById("count").getInnerText()); document.getElementById("increment").click(); assertEquals("1", client.getDocument().getElementById("count").getInnerText()); } } The exact document helper methods depend on the element you are testing. The mental model is stable: inspect the rendered document, perform the interaction a user would perform, then inspect the updated document or an injected collaborator. Manual integration context You can also write XIS integration tests without @XisBootTest . This is useful for very explicit tests, for builds that do not use the starter dependency, or for cases where the test wants complete control over the registered classes and instances. package example.products; import one.xis.context.IntegrationTestContext; import org.junit.jupiter.api.BeforeEach; import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test; import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertEquals; class ProductPageManualTest { private IntegrationTestContext context; private ProductService productService; @BeforeEach void setUp() { productService = new ProductService(); context = IntegrationTestContext.builder() .withBasePackageClass(ProductPageManualTest.class) .withInstance(productService) .build(); } @Test void opensProductPage() { productService.add(new Product(42, "Desk")); var client = context.openPage("/products/42.html"); assertEquals("Desk", client.getDocument().getElementByTagName("h1").getInnerText()); } } This lower-level style is still a XIS integration test. It renders the page through the XIS controller/template lifecycle. It simply does not use the JUnit annotation as a convenience layer. What belongs where Test kind Use it for Unit test Services, repository-adjacent logic, formatters, validators. XIS integration test Pages, actions, validation, navigation, modal flows, drag and drop. Browser/E2E test Real browser behavior, CSS/JS integration, deployment-level confidence. Generated is not required There are three valid testing styles: Generate a starter with xisTests , then edit it. Write a hand-made @XisBootTest . Build an IntegrationTestContext manually without the annotation. The first style is fastest when starting a page. The second is concise for ordinary page and frontlet behavior. The third is best when the test setup itself is part of what you want to control. Documentation rule Every non-trivial code example should either live in an executable documentation project or be derived from code that is compiled and tested somewhere. The same discipline is useful in application documentation: executable examples age much better than copied snippets. The detailed reference for generated tests, document interaction, drag and drop, authentication, manual contexts, and test dependencies lives in the Examples and Tests guide.

title: Gradle plugin and tooling
text: Gradle Plugin - XIS Operations / Gradle plugin Gradle plugin and tooling The Gradle plugin is part of the user-facing workflow: templates, tests, validation, runnable jars, native builds, local runs, catalogs, and smoke tests belong here. Why the plugin exists XIS intentionally moves some work to build time. Templates can be validated, catalogs can be generated, JavaScript resources can be bundled, and native-image metadata can be prepared before the application starts. The plugin keeps that workflow repeatable and visible. plugins { id "java" id "one.xis.plugin" version "0.18.0" } repositories { mavenCentral() } dependencies { implementation "one.xis:xis-boot" } Main tasks Task Purpose xisTemplates Generate or prepare template-related build artifacts. xisTests Generate starter tests and test support where the project uses that workflow. xisValidate Validate templates and framework metadata early. xisJar Build a runnable XIS jar with the required runtime resources. xisRun Run the application locally in the development workflow. xisNativeCompile Build a GraalVM native executable with portable defaults. xisNativeCompileForHost Build a native executable optimized for the current host. ./gradlew xisTemplates xisTests ./gradlew xisValidate ./gradlew xisJar ./gradlew xisRun Validation belongs in the build Template mistakes should fail before deployment. This includes missing bindings, invalid framework attributes, and template structures that a browser would parse differently from what the framework expects. XIS should be strict here because otherwise errors move from build time into user interactions. Generated code and user code Generated runners and catalogs are implementation details. Users should normally configure dependencies and tasks, not call generated native runners directly. The public API is the Gradle task and the application class. Without the plugin The framework should remain understandable without Gradle magic, but advanced behavior is less convenient without the plugin. Projects that bypass the plugin must take over the equivalent catalog, resource, validation, and native metadata work themselves.

title: Aspects And Interface Advice
section: Complete Guide / Advanced / Aspects And Interface Advice
text: Aspects And Interface Advice - XIS Complete Guide / Advanced / Aspects And Interface Advice Aspects And Interface Advice Complete reference chapter for the public XIS behavior. Use it when the overview pages are too short and you need exact annotations, tags, code shape, and runtime rules. Aspects And Interface Advice Advanced topics XIS Boot has a small AOP-style mechanism for cross-cutting behavior. It is deliberately narrower than AspectJ or Spring AOP: advice is applied only to components that are used through an interface. XIS does not modify classes and does not use bytecode generation. This limitation is intentional. Interface proxies keep the runtime friendly to native-image builds, avoid bytecode and classloader surprises, produce clearer error messages, and keep object lifetimes simple for the garbage collector. The price is explicit architecture: code that should be advised must be used through an interface. Use this when a library or application needs behavior such as timing, transactions, locking, retries, or auditing around service method calls. Shape Create an annotation and mark that annotation with @UseAdvice : import one.xis.context.UseAdvice; import java.lang.annotation.Retention; import java.lang.annotation.RetentionPolicy; import java.lang.annotation.Target; import static java.lang.annotation.ElementType.METHOD; import static java.lang.annotation.ElementType.TYPE; @UseAdvice(TimedAdvice.class) @Target({TYPE, METHOD}) @Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME) @interface Timed { } The advice receives an AdviceInvocation . It can inspect the method, arguments, annotations, target object, and context, and then call proceed() . import one.xis.context.Advice; import one.xis.context.AdviceInvocation; class TimedAdvice implements Advice { @Override public Object around(AdviceInvocation invocation) throws Throwable { long start = System.nanoTime(); try { return invocation.proceed(); } finally { long millis = (System.nanoTime() - start) / 1_000_000; System.out.println(invocation.method().getName() + " took " + millis + " ms"); } } } Usage Apply the annotation to an implementation method and inject the service through its interface: interface CustomerService { void update(Customer customer); } import one.xis.context.Service; @Service class CustomerServiceImpl implements CustomerService { @Timed public void update(Customer customer) { // business logic } } @Service class CustomerPageService { private final CustomerService customerService; CustomerPageService(CustomerService customerService) { this.customerService = customerService; } } The annotation may also be placed on the implementation class or the service interface. Type-level advice applies to all methods that are called through the proxied interface. The original object remains the target for dependency injection, @Init , @Bean , @Scheduled , and other framework reflection. The object injected into interface consumers is a JDK proxy. Limits is not declared by such an interface, fails fast. A component must be used through an interface to be advised. XIS validates advice placement at context startup. Advice on a class without a usable interface, or on a method that Injecting the concrete implementation class bypasses advice. Self-invocation is not intercepted. A call like this.update(...) inside CustomerServiceImpl is a normal Java call. Advice classes currently need a no-argument constructor. For generated interface proxies such as repository proxies, see Custom proxies .

title: Custom JavaScript
text: Custom JavaScript - XIS Complete Guide / Advanced / Custom JavaScript Custom JavaScript Complete reference chapter for the public XIS behavior. Use it when the overview pages are too short and you need exact annotations, tags, code shape, and runtime rules. Custom JavaScript Do not load application JavaScript with ordinary

title: Custom Proxies
text: Custom Proxies - XIS Complete Guide / Advanced / Custom Proxies Custom Proxies Complete reference chapter for the public XIS behavior. Use it when the overview pages are too short and you need exact annotations, tags, code shape, and runtime rules. Custom Proxies Advanced topics Custom proxies are an advanced extension point for libraries that want to expose interface-based components without making application code construct them by hand. XIS SQL repositories use this mechanism: an interface is annotated, XIS finds the annotation, asks the configured factory for a proxy, and then injects the proxy like any other component. Most applications should use existing proxy-based modules such as xis-sql . Create your own proxy annotation when you are building reusable infrastructure: repositories for another datastore, generated clients, typed access to an external system, or a company library that should feel like a normal injected service. Shape Of A Proxy Extension A proxy extension has three parts: an interface that application code injects an annotation for those interfaces a ProxyFactory that creates the runtime implementation package com.example.audit; import one.xis.context.Proxy; import java.lang.annotation.ElementType; import java.lang.annotation.Retention; import java.lang.annotation.RetentionPolicy; import java.lang.annotation.Target; @Target(ElementType.TYPE) @Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME) @Proxy(factory = AuditClientProxyFactory.class) @interface AuditClient { } package com.example.audit; @AuditClient interface CustomerAudit { void changed(long customerId, String message); } package com.example.audit; import one.xis.context.ProxyFactory; class AuditClientProxyFactory implements ProxyFactory<CustomerAudit> { private final AuditTransport transport; AuditClientProxyFactory(AuditTransport transport) { this.transport = transport; } @Override public CustomerAudit createProxy(Class<CustomerAudit> interf) { return (customerId, message) -> transport.send(interf.getName(), customerId, message); } } The factory itself can use constructor injection. In XIS Boot it is created by the XIS context. In Spring Boot, XIS registers interfaces annotated with proxy annotations as Spring beans and lets Spring create the factory, so Spring beans such as DataSource , HTTP clients, or project services can be injected into the factory. Using The Proxy Once the interface is annotated, application code uses it like any other dependency: import one.xis.Service; @Service class CustomerService { private final CustomerAudit audit; CustomerService(CustomerAudit audit) { this.audit = audit; } void renameCustomer(long customerId, String name) { audit.changed(customerId, "renamed to " + name); } } The application does not call the factory directly and does not need a manual @Bean method for every interface. Interface Advice Proxies Ordinary context components can also be wrapped by interface advice. That is a separate advanced topic because it is about cross-cutting behavior around normal service methods, not about generated interface implementations. See Aspects and interface advice . Factory Name Variant If the factory type should not be loaded directly from the annotation, use factoryName : @Proxy(factoryName = "com.example.audit.AuditClientProxyFactory") @interface AuditClient { } This is useful for optional integrations where the annotation module should not directly depend on the implementation module. Spring Boot Notes XIS Spring scans the Spring Boot application packages for interfaces annotated with annotations that themselves carry @Proxy . For every matching interface it registers a Spring bean backed by the proxy factory. This means: constructor injection into Spring components works for custom proxies proxy factories can depend on Spring beans libraries can provide proxy annotations without requiring handwritten Spring configuration in every application If a factory depends on optional infrastructure, make sure that infrastructure is available in the Spring context. For example, SQL repository factories need a DataSource ; xis-spring supplies the default XIS SQL datasource when xis-sql is on the classpath and the application has not defined its own DataSource . When Not To Use This Do not use custom proxies to hide ordinary business services. If a class can simply be a @Service or @Component , keep it that way. Proxies are best for interface families where the implementation is generated, reflected, remote, or backed by metadata on the interface.

title: Advanced Guide
text: Advanced Guide - XIS Complete Guide / Advanced / Overview Advanced Guide Advanced topics are public features, but they are not the normal starting point for a XIS application. Use them when the application shape requires distribution, custom browser behavior, framework extension, or infrastructure integration. Microfrontend architecture Distributed pages and frontlets with one coherent browser application. Reusable web artifacts Library-provided controllers, replaceable templates, and reusable UI behavior. Aspects Native-image-friendly advice for framework and infrastructure concerns. Custom proxies Generated clients, repositories, and infrastructure extensions. SQL transactions Explicit transaction control for cases where repository operations need a shared boundary. Custom JavaScript Extension files, EL functions, and browser-side integrations. Integration-test browser The server-side browser model for testing rendered pages. Theme The optional XIS theme and generated standard UI structure.

title: Integration-Test Browser Model
section: Complete Guide / Advanced / Integration-Test Browser Model
text: Integration-Test Browser Model - XIS Complete Guide / Advanced / Integration-Test Browser Model Integration-Test Browser Model Complete reference chapter for the public XIS behavior. Use it when the overview pages are too short and you need exact annotations, tags, code shape, and runtime rules. Integration-Test Browser Model XIS integration tests run the XIS JavaScript runtime in GraalVM JavaScript, but they do not start a real browser. The test module provides a small Java implementation of common browser objects so tests can render templates, click elements, submit forms, navigate, and inspect the resulting DOM quickly. Use this environment for XIS behavior. Use E2E tests with a real browser when browser behavior itself is important. Because tests run under GraalVM, some JavaScript or host-object interactions may be less forgiving than a browser would be. Keep custom JavaScript strict and explicit: declare variables, avoid relying on accidental globals, avoid assigning to read-only properties, and prefer simple browser APIs that are listed below. Extension files are bundled as plain scripts, not as JavaScript modules. export and import are not supported in XIS custom JavaScript extension files. Available Global Objects The integration-test script exposes these browser-like globals: Global What It Provides document Parsed DOM tree for the current XIS page. window Browser window facade with location, history, scroll state, and references to document/storage/console. localStorage In-memory Storage implementation for the test client. sessionStorage In-memory Storage implementation for the test client. console log , debug , warn , and error . console.error fails the test. Node DOM node constants such as Node.ELEMENT_NODE . setTimeout Runs the callback after a delay in a Java thread. atob Base64 decoder. encodeURIComponent URL component encoder. XIS also binds internal helpers used by its own runtime, such as htmlToElement and backendBridge . Document document supports the operations most XIS integration tests need: document.querySelector('#save'); document.querySelectorAll('.item'); document.getElementById('name'); document.getElementsByTagName('input'); document.createElement('div'); document.createTextNode('Hello'); document.createComment('note'); document.createDocumentFragment(); The common properties below are also available: document.documentElement; document.body; document.head; document.title; document.cookie; document.location; document.defaultView; document.title and document.cookie can be read and written in JavaScript. Element And Node Elements support the DOM operations used by the XIS runtime and by typical integration tests: element.getAttribute('data-id'); element.setAttribute('data-id', '42'); element.hasAttribute('disabled'); element.removeAttribute('disabled'); element.appendChild(child); element.insertBefore(newNode, markerNode); element.removeChild(child); element.remove(); element.cloneNode(); element.click(); element.dispatchEvent(event); element.querySelector('input[name="email"]'); element.querySelectorAll('.error'); Common DOM properties are exposed through Java getters and setters, so JavaScript can use property syntax: element.innerText = 'Saved'; element.innerHTML = 'Saved'; element.textContent = 'Saved'; element.className = 'active selected'; element.innerText; element.innerHTML; element.textContent; element.localName; element.tagName; element.parentNode; element.firstChild; element.nextSibling; element.childNodes; element.classList; Form element implementations exist for input , textarea , select , and option . Drag and drop tests can use one.xis.test.dom.DragAndDrop . It dispatches dragstart , dragover , and drop events with one shared DataTransfer object. The test browser model implements the setData and getData methods used by XIS drag and drop actions. Window window contains: window.document; window.location; window.history; window.localStorage; window.sessionStorage; window.console; window.scrollTo(0, 0); window.scrollX; window.scrollY; window.addEventListener(...) exists as a no-op. It is present so browser-oriented code can be loaded, but it does not simulate the browser event loop. Location And History location supports the fields XIS navigation needs: location.pathname; location.href; location.origin; location.search; history supports: history.pushState(state, title, url); history.replaceState(state, title, url); history.back(); history.forward(); The Java test API also exposes inspection helpers such as the current state and history entries. Storage localStorage and sessionStorage support the normal methods: localStorage.setItem('key', 'value'); localStorage.getItem('key'); localStorage.removeItem('key'); Values are kept in memory for the test client. They are not browser storage and they do not persist outside the test context. What Is Not A Real Browser The integration-test browser model intentionally does not implement the complete Web Platform. In particular, do not rely on it for: CSS cascade, layout, flex/grid position, dimensions, computed styles, fonts, or media queries canvas, SVG rendering, images, video, audio, WebGL, or animation frames focus management, pointer/touch/keyboard event fidelity, bubbling/capturing semantics, or default browser actions real networking APIs such as fetch , XMLHttpRequest , WebSocket , or EventSource real cookie handling, cookie attributes, SameSite/Secure behavior, or browser privacy rules Web Components, Shadow DOM, mutation observers, intersection observers, resize observers, or performance APIs browser security behavior such as CORS, CSP, redirects, popup blocking, or third-party cookie policy execution behavior of dynamically inserted

title: Microfrontend Architecture
text: Microfrontend Architecture - XIS Complete Guide / Advanced / Microfrontend Architecture Microfrontend Architecture Complete reference chapter for the public XIS behavior. Use it when the overview pages are too short and you need exact annotations, tags, code shape, and runtime rules. Microfrontend Architecture Microfrontend Architecture means that a service does not only own business logic and data access; it can also own the frontend fragment that presents that part of the domain. In XIS, those fragments are pages and frontlets. A browser application can therefore be composed from more than one XIS runtime, for example a shell application that renders the main page while selected pages or frontlets are served by another process. Most applications do not need this. The normal mode is same-origin: the page, its frontlets, and all XIS endpoints come from the same host. Dependencies Add xis-distributed to each participating XIS application. For standalone XIS Boot: plugins { id "java" id "one.xis.plugin" version "0.18.0" } repositories { mavenCentral() } dependencies { implementation "one.xis:xis-boot" implementation "one.xis:xis-distributed" } For Spring Boot: plugins { id "java" id "org.springframework.boot" version "3.5.0" id "io.spring.dependency-management" version "1.1.7" id "one.xis.plugin" version "0.18.0" } repositories { mavenCentral() } dependencies { implementation "org.springframework.boot:spring-boot-starter-web" implementation "one.xis:xis-spring" implementation "one.xis:xis-distributed" } Routing Model Each participating XIS runtime still exposes its normal /xis/config . The shell additionally asks its own runtime for the distributed host list and then loads /xis/config from every listed host. The browser merges those configs. After merging: local pages and frontlets still use same-origin requests pages and frontlets from a remote config use that remote host page and frontlet ownership comes from the runtime that exposes the config There are no page or frontlet mappings in application.properties . Properties Configuration For simple XIS Boot and Spring Boot deployments, configure the remote host list in application.properties : xis.distributed.hosts=https://components.example.com,https://checkout.example.com Hosts must include protocol and host, for example http://localhost:9000 or https://checkout.example.com . The host list is interpreted from the point of view of the current runtime. In a shell application it lists the remote runtimes whose /xis/config should be loaded. If no separate CORS origins are configured, the same list is also used as the list of browser origins that may call this runtime. Use xis.distributed.allowed-origins when config discovery and CORS are not the same list. A remote runtime commonly lists the shell as an allowed origin, even though the shell is not a remote component host for that runtime: xis.distributed.hosts=https://components.example.com,https://checkout.example.com xis.distributed.allowed-origins=https://app.example.com When xis-distributed is on the classpath, this property is required unless the application provides its own XisDistributedConfig implementation. Java Configuration For dynamic XIS Boot deployments, implement XisDistributedConfig as a normal XIS component: import one.xis.context.Component; import one.xis.distributed.XisDistributedConfig; import java.util.List; @Component class DistributedConfig implements XisDistributedConfig { @Override public List<String> getHosts() { return List.of("https://components.example.com", "https://checkout.example.com"); } } For Spring Boot, provide XisDistributedConfig as a Spring bean: import one.xis.distributed.XisDistributedConfig; import org.springframework.context.annotation.Bean; import org.springframework.context.annotation.Configuration; import java.util.List; @Configuration class DistributedConfiguration { @Bean XisDistributedConfig xisDistributedConfig() { return () -> List.of("https://components.example.com", "https://checkout.example.com"); } } Override getAllowedOrigins() when the browser origins allowed by CORS differ from the remote hosts used for config discovery: import one.xis.context.Component; import one.xis.distributed.XisDistributedConfig; import java.util.List; import java.util.Set; @Component class RemoteDistributedConfig implements XisDistributedConfig { @Override public List<String> getHosts() { return List.of("https://components.example.com", "https://checkout.example.com"); } @Override public Set<String> getAllowedOrigins() { return Set.of("https://app.example.com"); } } Return stable host values. Future versions may use the same contract for host selection or availability checks. Navigation By URL In same-origin applications, returning a page class or PageResponse is often the most convenient navigation style. In distributed applications, URLs and strings are usually the better boundary between applications. This applies to pages and frontlets. The reason is classpath ownership. If an action in the shell application returns a remote page class, the shell application must have that remote application class on its classpath. That couples the applications at Java level. If the action returns a URL string instead, the contract is only the public page URL. The browser resolves that URL to the page entry in the merged client config and uses the host that provided that page. import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/cart.html") class CartPage { @Action String checkout() { return "/checkout.html"; } } If /checkout.html belongs to the config loaded from https://checkout.example.com , rendering and later page actions for that page go to https://checkout.example.com .
