A file input is submitted as multipart form data. The matching Java form field or action parameter must be annotated with @Upload ; see File uploads for supported Java types and upload limits. When multiple controls use the same binding, XIS submits all selected values for that binding. Use that for checkbox groups or any repeated form value that should arrive as a collection: Equivalent element syntax: class ProductForm { private List tags; List getTags() { return tags; } void setTags(List tags) { this.tags = tags; } } Validation Messages Use xis:message-for for field messages. Element syntax: Use global messages for validation errors that are not attached to one field. These are still validation messages from the current form result, not the general system-error area: When messages are present, XIS inserts a generated
      with one
    • per message into the element. Style .error or use selectors such as xis\:global-messages ul.error when you want to target the generated list specifically. General server errors are rendered by the browser-side message handler into an element with id="system-messages" when that element exists on the page. Use xis:error-class together with xis:binding when a field or label should receive a CSS class on validation error: Use xis:error-binding when the element should react to a field error but is not itself bound as a form control. Use xis:error-style when an inline style should be applied instead of, or in addition to, a CSS class: xis:error-binding is evaluated relative to the current form binding, just like xis:message-for . The style or class is applied while the last form action has a validation message for that field. Dynamic Classes Use xis:selection-class and xis:selection-group for selection styling when XIS should manage the selected CSS class. Put xis:selection-group on the surrounding element and xis:selection-class on each selectable child. XIS keeps one selected element per group.
      Products Orders
      For HTML preview tools you can also write the attributes without the xis: prefix. XIS normalizes both forms:
      Products Orders
      Client State Client-side storage binding is available, but it is not required for normal server-backed applications. Use it only when state should intentionally live in the browser.
      ${defaultValue(localStorage.cart.count, '0')}
      Element syntax wraps the content that should be refreshed when that store changes: ${defaultValue(localStorage.cart.count, '0')} Supported stores are localStorage , sessionStorage , and clientState . XIS does not send the whole browser store to the server. It sends the keys that the current page or frontlet declares through controller parameters such as @LocalStorage("cart") or @SessionStorage("wizard") . Storage parameters are written back after an action, so mutable DTO-like values can be updated in the action and persisted in the browser. Raw Content xis:raw inserts its content without normal XIS handling. Use it rarely, for content that is already trusted and already prepared as HTML. Trusted HTML Use text="true" to insert the raw content as plain text: Shown as text Element Required Attributes Some framework elements need one or more attributes so XIS can bind them to a controller method, model value, or target container. The template validator reports these cases during the build. Element Required attributes One of page , frontlet , or modal action One of page , frontlet , modal , or action binding var and array binding name container-id condition name binding message-for name ; value is optional because the element text can be used as the value binding binding store , one of localStorage , sessionStorage , or clientState action binding and do not require attributes. supports optional text="true" when the content should be inserted as plain text instead of trusted HTML. Quick Reference Behavior Attribute syntax Element syntax Conditional rendering xis:if="condition" Iterate content xis:foreach="item:items" Repeat element xis:repeat="item:items" use around the element Page link Page button

title: Template Location And Mapping
section: Complete Guide / Template Location And Mapping
text: Template Location And Mapping - XIS Complete Guide / Template Location And Mapping Template Location And Mapping Complete reference chapter for the public XIS behavior. Use it when the overview pages are too short and you need exact annotations, tags, code shape, and runtime rules. Template Location And Mapping Every page, frontlet, and include has Java code and an HTML template. XIS looks for the template by convention first, and you only need an annotation when you want to override that convention. Default Location By default, put the HTML file next to the Java controller in the same package: src/main/java/ example/products/ ProductPage.java ProductPage.html For a controller class named ProductPage , XIS looks for ProductPage.html in the same package. This works for files kept under src/main/java because the XIS Gradle plugin copies HTML templates into the application resources during the build. This side-by-side layout is the recommended XIS style. Most changes touch the controller and template together, and the project tree immediately shows which Java class owns which HTML file. During development, XIS resolves side-by-side templates from src/main/java before it falls back to copied build resources. When an HTML template is changed on disk, the next browser request reparses it if the file timestamp changed. That means normal template edits usually only need a browser reload, not a Java restart or a Gradle resource-copy step. The same package can also be mirrored under src/main/resources : src/main/resources/ example/products/ ProductPage.html Use the side-by-side src/main/java style when you want the controller and template to be easy to find together. Use src/main/resources when your project convention keeps all resource files there. If the same resource path exists in both source roots, src/main/java wins. This keeps the controller-near template as the active development file. Generate Missing Templates And Tests The XIS Gradle plugin and tools can generate missing template files for page and frontlet controllers: ./gradlew xisTemplates The generated files are only a starting point. They are written to the package of the Java controller, so the project tree shows which template belongs to which controller. It is usually better to write the controller skeleton before running the generator. If the controller already contains its @ModelData , @FormData , and @Action methods, xisTemplates can create a more useful template: model data is rendered with expressions or xis:foreach , form data becomes a bound form with validation message placeholders, and actions become buttons. In other words, the generated template is not just based on the controller class name; it tries to mirror the controller methods that define the page contract. For example, a controller with a @FormData("customer") method and a matching save action will produce a form using xis:binding="customer" , field bindings, error bindings, and a submit button. You still edit the generated HTML, but you start with the important XIS wiring already in place. The same plugin can generate missing integration-test skeletons for page controllers: ./gradlew xisTests The generated tests are written under src/test/java in the same package as the page controller. They use @XisBootTest , register the page controller in the XIS test context, and open the page by URL. The XIS Gradle plugin adds the required test starter automatically; do not add xis-test or xis-boot-starter-test again in a normal plugin-based build. Existing files are never overwritten, so both tasks are safe to run repeatedly while you add controllers. You can run both tasks together after creating a page class: ./gradlew xisTemplates xisTests That gives you the two files a new page usually needs next: the HTML template and a first executable test. For best results, first sketch the controller methods that should exist, then generate the template and test, and then refine both files into the final behavior. Explicit Template With @HtmlFile Use @HtmlFile when the template name or location should not follow the default convention. The annotation works for templates copied from src/main/java and for templates kept directly in src/main/resources ; at runtime both are classpath resources. package example.products; import one.xis.HtmlFile; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/products.html") @HtmlFile("ProductList.html") class ProductPage { } A relative @HtmlFile path is resolved from the controller package. The example above looks for: example/products/ProductList.html An absolute path starts with / and is resolved from the classpath resource root: package example.products; import one.xis.HtmlFile; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/products.html") @HtmlFile("/templates/ProductList.html") class ProductPage { } This looks for: templates/ProductList.html Use an absolute path when a template should live in a shared folder independent of the controller package. Use a relative path when the template should still be organized near the controller package, but should not use the controller class name. Shared Templates Several controllers can use the same template by pointing them to the same @HtmlFile . package example.products; import one.xis.HtmlFile; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/products.html") @HtmlFile("/templates/catalog.html") class ProductPage { } package example.offers; import one.xis.HtmlFile; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/offers.html") @HtmlFile("/templates/catalog.html") class OfferPage { } This is useful when different controllers expose the same model shape and should render with the same HTML. Default Templates @DefaultHtmlFile is mainly useful for reusable library controllers that provide a framework default while still allowing an application to override the template with @HtmlFile . See Reusable web artifacts for the full pattern. package example.library; import one.xis.DefaultHtmlFile; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/profile.html") @DefaultHtmlFile("/default-profile.html") class ProfilePage { } Application code normally uses the default convention or @HtmlFile .

title: Template Syntax
text:
Template Syntax - XIS Complete Guide / Template Syntax Template Syntax Complete reference chapter for the public XIS behavior. Use it when the overview pages are too short and you need exact annotations, tags, code shape, and runtime rules. Template Syntax XIS templates are plain HTML with XIS attributes, XIS tags, and expression language. Pages use complete HTML documents. Frontlets use template fragments. Many XIS template features can be written in two equivalent styles: Attribute syntax keeps the markup close to ordinary HTML and usually previews better in design tools. Element syntax uses explicit xis:* framework elements and can be preferable when you want a more XML-like template. This documentation shows both styles where the choice matters. For a compact side-by-side reference, see Tags and attributes . Expression Language Expressions use ${...} .

${product.name}

${product.description}

${count > 0 ? count + " items" : "No items"} Expressions can access model data, loop variables, action parameters, and selected framework context values. They can be used in text content and in ordinary HTML attributes. XIS does not give HTML attributes such as data-id special meaning; it only replaces the ${...} expression in the attribute value.
  • ${product.name}
    • For attributes that make the browser load a resource immediately, use the xis:* form so the browser does not request the raw template expression before XIS has initialized the page: XIS removes xis:src during initialization and writes the evaluated value to the real src attribute. Client-Side Messages XIS loads the resolved message bundle during application startup and exposes it in templates as messages .

    ${messages.customerListTitle}

    Use dot notation for simple keys and bracket notation for keys that contain dots or are computed dynamically. Messages come from the same classpath property files that validation uses: # messages.properties customerListTitle=Customers customer.form.save=Save customer Locale-specific files use the Java language suffix, for example messages_de.properties . Application messages*.properties files override the framework default-messages*.properties fallbacks with the same rules as validation messages. The documented public EL surface is: literals: strings, numbers, booleans, null , and undefined model values, loop variables, action parameters, and selected framework context values property and index access with dot and bracket syntax arithmetic, comparison, boolean operators, parentheses, and ternary expressions built-in EL functions listed below, including nested function calls text interpolation and attribute interpolation with ${...} Property and Index Access Use dot notation for ordinary Java bean properties and bracket notation when the key or index is dynamic.

    ${user.name}

    ${user['name']}

    ${items[1]}

    ${items[index + 1]}

    ${lookup[selectedKey]}

    ${lookup[prefix + '_item'].label}

    Bracket expressions are useful when a map key is built from other model values. The result can be chained with normal property access, as in ${lookup[prefix + '_item'].label} . Operators and Conditions Template expressions support arithmetic, comparison, boolean operators, parentheses, and ternary expressions.

    ${price * quantity}

    ${count > 0 ? count + " items" : "No items"}

    Content
    Useful examples:

    ${toUpperCase(user.name)}

    ${default(user.email, "No email")}

    ${round(order.total, 2)}

    ${formatDate(order.createdAt, "en-US")}

    Function parameters can be expressions, and function calls can be nested:

    ${defaultValue(user.displayName, toUpperCase(user.login))}

    ${join(split(tagsText, ","), " / ")}

    Keep complex business rules in Java @ModelData methods. Use template expressions for presentation logic. Built-in EL Functions XIS includes a set of expression-language functions for common template work. These are built into the browser runtime. String functions: Function Use toUpperCase(str) Converts a string to uppercase. toLowerCase(str) Converts a string to lowercase. trim(str) Removes leading and trailing whitespace. substring(str, start, end) Returns part of a string. replace(str, search, replacement) Replaces the first matching string fragment. split(str, separator) Splits a string into an array. join(array, separator) Joins an array into a string. Collection and object functions: Function Use length(value) Returns length or size for strings, arrays, maps, sets, or objects. size(value) Alias for length(value) . count(value) Alias for length(value) . empty(value) Returns true for null , empty strings, empty arrays, empty maps, or empty sets. isEmpty(value) Alias for empty(value) . notEmpty(value) Returns true for non-empty strings, arrays, maps, or sets. contains(container, value) Checks strings, arrays, or object values. keys(object) Returns object keys. values(object) Returns object values. hasKey(object, key) Checks whether an object owns a key. flatMap(value, path) Extracts nested values from one object or an array of objects. filter(array, property, value) Filters an array by property equality. arrayOf(first, last) Creates an integer array from first to last , inclusive. Number functions: Function Use round(value) Rounds to the nearest integer. round(value, digits) Rounds to the given number of decimal places. floor(value) Rounds down. ceil(value) Rounds up. abs(value) Returns the absolute value. sum(...) Adds numeric values or numeric values inside arrays. Date and time functions: Function Use formatDate(date, locale) Formats a date with medium date style. formatDateTime(date, locale) Formats date and time with medium date style and short time style. formatTime(date, locale) Formats time with short time style. year(date) Extracts the year. month(date) Extracts the month number, starting at 1 . day(date) Extracts the day of month. hour(date) Extracts the hour. minute(date) Extracts the minute. Fallback functions: Function Use default(value, fallback) Returns fallback when value is null , an empty string, or an empty array. defaultValue(value, fallback) Alias for default(value, fallback) . Security display functions: Function Use isUserInRole(role) Returns true when the current authenticated user has the named role. isUserInRoles(role, ...) Returns true when the current authenticated user has at least one of the named roles. Also accepts an array. These functions run in the browser and are only for presentation logic, usually with xis:if . They can hide or show controls, but hidden frontend markup is not access control. Protect the page, frontlet, action, parameter, or DTO with @Authenticated or @Roles whenever the operation itself must be restricted. Examples:

    ${default(product.name, "Unnamed product")}

    ${toUpperCase(user.lastName)}, ${user.firstName}

    ${count(products)} products

    Products are available.

    Total: ${round(sum(order.lines), 2)}

    Created: ${formatDateTime(order.createdAt, "en-US")}

    Custom EL functions are an advanced extension topic. Project code can register browser-side functions with XIS.addElFunction(name, functionRef) before templates using that function are initialized. See Custom JavaScript and custom EL functions when you need project-specific template functions. Iteration Use xis:foreach to repeat the content of an element. Attribute syntax:
    • ${productIndex + 1}. ${product.name}
    In attribute syntax, the content of the host element is repeated. The host element itself is not repeated. Element syntax:
    • ${productIndex + 1}. ${product.name}
      •
    XIS adds an index variable by appending Index to the loop variable name. In the example above, the index is productIndex . Use element syntax or xis:repeat when attributes of the repeated element depend on the loop item:
  • ${product.name}
    • Conditions Attribute syntax:

    Products are available.

    No products found.

    Element syntax:

    Products are available.

     Expressions can often be written without ${...} when the attribute expects an expression:

    Products are available.

    Navigation Use xis:page for XIS navigation. Attribute syntax: Products Element syntax: Products Use xis:action for controller actions. Action Button: Actionlink: Save Framework action element: Save Pass parameters with xis:parameter : Simple drag-and-drop actions use xis:drag and xis:drop : Piece
    Target
    @Action void move(@ActionParameter("from") String from, @ActionParameter("target") String target) { } Read Drag and drop for the full behavior and test support. Frontlets and Includes Short frontlet tag: Container attribute syntax:
    Includes: Include attribute syntax:
    The include value is a registered include key, not a free resource path. Only fragments exposed by an @Include controller can be referenced from templates. This is intentional: application code has to explicitly allow a fragment to be included, and templates cannot browse to arbitrary HTML resources. Includes are initialized inside the surrounding page or frontlet. They are for markup reuse, but the included markup may still contain XIS links, action buttons, parameters, and model expressions that belong to that surrounding controller. Use frontlets when the fragment needs its own controller, model data, or independently replaceable state. Frontlet container element syntax: Attribute syntax:
    Forms Form fields use xis:binding . Attribute syntax:
    Framework element syntax: Save Validation messages:
    Static Assets Static resources such as CSS, images, and fonts belong under: src/main/resources/public/ Reference them without /public : JavaScript needs special care in XIS because pages and frontlets are inserted dynamically in an SPA-style runtime. Avoid adding ordinary

title: Cloud native and native images
text: Cloud Native - XIS Operations / Cloud native Cloud native and native images XIS can build JVM applications and GraalVM native executables. Native images are mainly about startup, memory, and deployment shape. Throughput-sensitive services should still be benchmarked in their target environment. Native build tasks The normal native task uses conservative defaults that work across machines. xisNativeCompileForHost is meant for deployments where the executable is built on the same CPU family on which it will run. It can pass host-oriented compiler options without making that the default for every build pipeline. Optimization level should be configurable, but the default should remain aligned with GraalVM's documented recommendation. Users who care about a specific service must benchmark their actual workload. dependencies { implementation "one.xis:xis-boot-native" } ./gradlew xisNativeCompile ./gradlew xisNativeCompileForHost ./gradlew xisNativeCompileForHost -PxisNativeImageArgs="-O3" Native metadata Native applications use generated catalogs and generated metadata instead of relying on runtime classpath scanning. This is a major part of the XIS native value: users should not have to hand-maintain reflection, proxy, resource, and component metadata. import one.xis.boot.XISBootApplication; @XISBootApplication public class Application { } The plugin uses the application class to generate the native entry point and the metadata catalogs. Application code does not call the generated native runner directly. What native is good at Native images are attractive for fast startup, lower memory use, small service processes, command-line helpers, and environments where many JVM processes would otherwise be too expensive. These are real operational benefits for XIS Boot applications and pure HTTP Controller services. What native does not promise A native executable is not automatically faster for long-running server throughput. The JVM can optimize hot code while the application is running, and that can beat compile-time optimization in some workloads. Choose native for startup time, memory footprint, packaging, and operational shape; benchmark long-running throughput in the target environment before treating native as the faster option. Native database modules Native images need database drivers and driver metadata at build time. On the JVM, a JDBC driver can often be a late runtimeOnly dependency and still be discovered through DriverManager or service loading. A native image is closed during the build: driver classes, service registrations, resources, reflection needs, and initialization behavior must already be reachable. Add the normal persistence module plus the native driver module for the database used by the application: dependencies { implementation "one.xis:xis-boot-native" implementation "one.xis:xis-sql" implementation "one.xis:xis-boot-native-postgresql" } The available native SQL driver modules are xis-boot-native-h2 , xis-boot-native-postgresql , and xis-boot-native-mariadb . For MongoDB applications use xis-boot-native-mongodb together with the MongoDB repository module. dependencies { implementation "one.xis:xis-boot-native" implementation "one.xis:xis-mongodb" implementation "one.xis:xis-boot-native-mongodb" } The complete guide has the full dependency matrix and background in Database Modules . Reflection, records, and inner classes Native-image support must include the classes used by controllers, DTOs, nested DTOs, records, validation objects, and response models. Inner classes matter because they often appear naturally in small page controllers and form models. The generated metadata must walk that structure instead of assuming one public top-level class per file. HTTP services The HTTP Controller API is an especially clean native-image target. A small service can depend on the HTTP runtime, validation, and its own business code without shipping the page framework JavaScript resources. If an application uses XIS pages, those resources are included because the browser runtime needs them. dependencies { implementation "one.xis:xis-http-controller-native" }

title: Development workflow
text:
Development workflow - XIS Operations / Development workflow Development workflow XIS is designed for a short backend-oriented development loop: write the controller, generate a starting template or test when useful, validate templates early, and keep executable tests next to the feature. Start with a page controller A XIS page starts with a controller annotated with @Page . The controller describes the URL, the model data, the form data, and the actions the browser can call. If these annotations are new to you, keep the Annotation reference , Pages and templates , and Forms and validation open while reading this example. This small contact page intentionally contains a few things that the template generator can reflect later: a scalar model value, an array model value, a form object, validation annotations, and a form action. package example.contact; import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.FormData; import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; import one.xis.validation.EMail; import one.xis.validation.Mandatory; @Page("/contact.html") class ContactPage { @ModelData("headline") String headline() { return "Contact us"; } @ModelData("topics") String[] topics() { return new String[] { "Support", "Sales", "Partnership" }; } @FormData("message") ContactMessage message() { return new ContactMessage("", "", "Support"); } @Action void send(@FormData("message") ContactMessage message) { } record ContactMessage( @Mandatory String name, @Mandatory @EMail String email, @Mandatory String topic ) { } } @ModelData exposes values to the template. @FormData creates the server-side form object. @Action marks a method that a button or form can call. @Mandatory and @EMail are server-side validation annotations. The browser can show validation feedback, but the server remains authoritative. Generate the template Run xisTemplates after the controller shape exists. The task creates missing template files next to the controller. Existing templates are not overwritten. ./gradlew xisTemplates For the controller above, the task generated this actual file:
${headline}
  • ${topic}
Notice how the generator mirrored the controller contract: headline became an expression, topics became a xis:foreach list, message became a form binding, the record components became inputs, and send became the form action. The result is not meant to be beautiful. It is meant to be executable wiring that you can now refine. For template rules, see Template syntax and Tags and attributes . Generate the first test The same controller can also get a starter integration test. In this probe project, running xisTemplates and xisTests as separate commands worked reliably: ./gradlew xisTests The task generated this actual test: package example.contact; import one.xis.boot.test.XisBootTest; import one.xis.context.IntegrationTestContext; import one.xis.test.InTestContext; import org.junit.jupiter.api.Test; import static org.junit.jupiter.api.Assertions.assertNotNull; /** * Integration test for ContactPage * @generated */ @XisBootTest class ContactPageTest { private IntegrationTestContext context; @InTestContext private ContactPage contactPage; @Test void test() { // Edit url if you need path variables or query parameters var client = context.openPage("/contact.html"); assertNotNull(client.getDocument()); // TODO: Add more assertions } } @XisBootTest creates a XIS integration-test context. IntegrationTestContext opens a page the same way a browser would enter it. @InTestContext gives the test access to the managed page object. The generated assertion only proves that a document was rendered. From here, edit the test until it says what the page must really do: show the headline, render the topic list, submit invalid data, show validation messages, and call the action for valid data. See Testing from the start and Examples and tests . Validate before running After generation and after each meaningful template edit, run validation. It catches structural mistakes before you start the application: missing required framework attributes, invalid bindings, unknown xis:* elements, and model/form properties that do not match the controller contract. ./gradlew xisValidate ./gradlew xisValidate --all-errors Validation is not a replacement for tests. It proves that the template is structurally consistent with the server contract; tests prove that the feature behaves correctly. Run locally XIS Boot applications can be run through the Gradle plugin. Pass a port when the default port is already in use. ./gradlew xisRun --port=9090 In XIS Boot development runs, public resources under src/main/resources/public are read from the source file when available. CSS and similar resource edits should therefore become visible after pressing the browser reload button. Packaged jars remain static, and Java/controller changes still require rebuilding or restarting the application. The loop in practice The practical rhythm is: ./gradlew xisTemplates ./gradlew xisTests ./gradlew xisValidate ./gradlew test ./gradlew xisRun --port=9090 Then edit Java, templates, and resources together. Use browser reload for public resource changes, rerun xisValidate when template structure changes, and keep the test suite moving with the feature. Related pages The detailed task reference is in Gradle plugin and tooling . The testing introduction is in Testing from the start , and the full test API is covered by Examples and tests .

title: Gateway configuration
text: Gateway configuration - XIS Operations / Gateway configuration Gateway configuration A XIS Boot application usually runs behind a gateway. Apache or nginx can terminate HTTPS, serve static pages, and forward the application paths to the local XIS process. What must be proxied XIS pages may be mounted below an application path such as /app-wizzard/ , but the framework runtime also uses root-level resources and service endpoints. A gateway therefore has to forward the page path and the XIS runtime paths together. For a page application behind /app-wizzard/ , the important paths are: Path Purpose /app-wizzard/ The application page and its public resources. /xis/ XIS internal endpoints, including server-sent events such as /xis/events . /bundle.min.js The XIS browser runtime used by page applications. /default-main.css Small framework CSS for XIS-owned browser elements. Apache 2 Enable the proxy modules once and keep the application process bound to localhost. The example assumes the XIS application listens on 127.0.0.1:8081 . sudo a2enmod proxy proxy_http sudo systemctl reload apache2 ProxyPreserveHost On ProxyPass /app-wizzard/ http://127.0.0.1:8081/app-wizzard/ ProxyPassReverse /app-wizzard/ http://127.0.0.1:8081/app-wizzard/ ProxyPass /xis/ http://127.0.0.1:8081/xis/ ProxyPassReverse /xis/ http://127.0.0.1:8081/xis/ ProxyPass /bundle.min.js http://127.0.0.1:8081/bundle.min.js ProxyPassReverse /bundle.min.js http://127.0.0.1:8081/bundle.min.js ProxyPass /default-main.css http://127.0.0.1:8081/default-main.css ProxyPassReverse /default-main.css http://127.0.0.1:8081/default-main.css Validate before reloading: sudo apache2ctl configtest sudo systemctl reload apache2 nginx nginx uses location blocks for the same split. SSE endpoints need buffering disabled so events reach the browser immediately. server { listen 443 ssl; server_name example.com; root /var/www/html/xis-docs; index index.html; location /app-wizzard/ { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8081/app-wizzard/; proxy_set_header Host $host; proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme; proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for; } location /xis/ { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8081/xis/; proxy_set_header Host $host; proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme; proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for; proxy_buffering off; } location = /bundle.min.js { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8081/bundle.min.js; proxy_set_header Host $host; } location = /default-main.css { proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:8081/default-main.css; proxy_set_header Host $host; } } sudo nginx -t sudo systemctl reload nginx Static documentation beside an app Static documentation and a XIS application can share one host. Let the gateway serve static HTML directly and only forward the application and XIS runtime paths. That keeps documentation deploys independent from application deploys. When Apache Server Side Includes are used for static documentation, keep SSI on the documentation directory and keep the XIS application behind proxy rules. The two concerns should not be mixed inside the application process.

title: Scheduled jobs
text: Scheduled Jobs - XIS Operations / Scheduled jobs Scheduled jobs Scheduled jobs are a small but useful public feature. This chapter covers fixed delay, fixed rate, the scheduler component, and typical runtime implications. Fixed delay and fixed rate A fixed-delay job waits after one run has finished before scheduling the next. A fixed-rate job aims for a regular cadence. The difference matters when work can take longer than expected, so the code examples show both variants instead of treating them as interchangeable timers. import one.xis.context.Component; import one.xis.context.Scheduled; import java.util.concurrent.TimeUnit; @Component class ReminderJob { @Scheduled(initialDelay = 1, fixedDelay = 1, timeUnit = TimeUnit.MINUTES) void sendDueReminders() { reminderService.sendDueReminders(); } } @Scheduled(initialDelay = 5, fixedRate = 10, timeUnit = TimeUnit.SECONDS) void pollExternalStatus() { externalStatusService.poll(); } Scheduler components Scheduled work should live in ordinary components with explicit dependencies. That keeps jobs testable and avoids static utility code that is hard to replace in integration tests. Interaction with UI refresh A scheduled job can publish refresh events after it changes state. It should not talk directly to browser-specific structures. The browser should learn about the change through the same event and refresh model as any other server-side update.

title: Extension points
text: Extension Points - XIS Reference / Extension points Extension points Advanced extension points include aspects, interface advice, custom proxies, request filters, HTTP filters, custom JavaScript, and custom EL functions. Use extension points sparingly Extension points exist so applications and libraries can integrate with the framework without forking it. They are not meant to be the first solution for normal page behavior. Prefer ordinary pages, actions, repositories, and services until the repeated pattern is clear. Filters HTTP filters are the right place for cross-cutting request handling such as static resource shortcuts, diagnostic headers, or special integration endpoints. Page logic should stay in page controllers. import one.xis.context.Component; import one.xis.http.FilterChain; import one.xis.http.HttpFilter; import one.xis.http.HttpRequest; import one.xis.http.HttpResponse; import java.util.UUID; @Component class RequestIdFilter implements HttpFilter { @Override public void doFilter(HttpRequest request, HttpResponse response, FilterChain chain) { response.addHeader("X-Request-Id", UUID.randomUUID().toString()); chain.doFilter(request, response); } } Custom EL functions Custom expression-language functions can make templates cleaner when a formatting or calculation rule is repeated. They should remain deterministic and presentation-oriented. Business decisions belong in model data or controller methods. # src/main/resources/META-INF/xis/js/extensions js/template-functions.js XIS.addElFunction('initials', function(name) { return String(name || '') .trim() .split(/\s+/) .map(part => part.charAt(0).toUpperCase()) .join(''); }); ${initials(customer.name)} Proxies and aspects Proxy and advice mechanisms are advanced integration tools. They are useful for infrastructure concerns, but they can also make control flow harder to read. Reach for them after the ordinary component model is clear, not as the default way to structure application code. import one.xis.context.Advice; import one.xis.context.AdviceInvocation; import one.xis.context.UseAdvice; import java.lang.annotation.Retention; import java.lang.annotation.RetentionPolicy; @UseAdvice(TimedAdvice.class) @Retention(RetentionPolicy.RUNTIME) @interface Timed { } class TimedAdvice implements Advice { @Override public Object around(AdviceInvocation invocation) throws Throwable { long start = System.nanoTime(); try { return invocation.proceed(); } finally { System.out.println(invocation.method().getName() + " took " + (System.nanoTime() - start) + " ns"); } } } Replaceable defaults Framework modules and reusable libraries can provide a fallback implementation with @DefaultComponent . If the application provides a normal component for the same type, the normal component wins. This is useful when a module should work out of the box but still be easy to replace. import one.xis.context.DefaultComponent; @DefaultComponent class DefaultMailSender implements MailSender { @Override public void send(Message message) { // framework or library default } } import one.xis.context.Component; @Component class SmtpMailSender implements MailSender { @Override public void send(Message message) { // application-specific implementation } } The replacement rule also applies to injected collections and to @Bean methods declared on a replaced default component. That keeps defaults from leaking into the runtime after the application has supplied a real implementation.

title: Language support
text: Language Support - XIS Reference / Language support Language support Java is the canonical documentation path. Kotlin and Groovy are supported, but their chapters should be honest about requirements, native-image limitations, and tested behavior. Java as the main path The main documentation examples use Java. That keeps the reference path predictable and matches the strongest test and usage focus. import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/hello.html") class HelloPage { @ModelData("message") String message() { return "Hello from Java"; } } Kotlin Kotlin controllers and form objects are part of the supported surface. Kotlin is also relevant for native images, because Java and Kotlin are the intended native application languages. import one.xis.ModelData import one.xis.Page @Page("/hello-kotlin.html") class HelloKotlinPage { @ModelData("message") fun message(): String = "Hello from Kotlin" } Groovy Groovy is supported on the JVM path and has tests, but it is not the main development path. XIS documents that distinction explicitly so Groovy is neither overstated as the main path nor treated as accidental support. import one.xis.ModelData import one.xis.Page @Page("/hello-groovy.html") class HelloGroovyPage { @ModelData("message") String message() { "Hello from Groovy" } }

title: Security
text: Security - XIS Security / Security model Security This overview explains the security model at a high level. The full security reference covers roles, template visibility, ownership checks, local authentication, external OpenID Connect providers, TOTP, XIS as IDP, and distributed SSO. Need the full API? Continue with the complete security guide for configuration, login flows, role checks, external identity providers, and SSO. Application security The ordinary security story is application-level security: pages and actions can require roles, templates can hide or show content based on authorization, DTOs can carry role metadata, and ownership checks can decide whether a user may access a particular object. Roles come from the authenticated user context. In a local-login application they are supplied by the local credential/user service. With an external OpenID Connect provider they are mapped from the authenticated identity and its claims. Once the user is authenticated, page and action checks read those roles through the XIS security context. import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; import one.xis.Roles; import one.xis.UserId; @Page("/account.html") @Roles("USER") class AccountPage { @ModelData("message") String message(@UserId String userId) { return "Account for " + userId; } @Action @Roles("ADMIN") void rebuildIndex() { adminService.rebuildIndex(); } } <button xis:if="isUserInRole('ADMIN')" xis:action="rebuildIndex"> Rebuild index </button> Template visibility is only a usability rule. The action still needs @Roles or @Authenticated , because browser HTML is never the security boundary. See roles on pages, actions, and frontlets and template visibility for the complete behavior. Login and identity providers XIS supports local authentication, custom login templates, TOTP two-factor login, and external OpenID Connect providers. These topics belong in the security chapter because most users need to understand them before they care about distributed architecture. The complete guide explains local authentication , external OpenID Connect providers , and login template customization. SSO and XIS as identity provider Distributed SSO and XIS as an OpenID Connect provider are architectural security topics. They should be explained here first and then cross-linked from the microfrontend chapter where topology and cross-application composition become relevant. See XIS as OpenID Connect provider and distributed SSO for the detailed setup.

title: Custom JavaScript
text: Custom JavaScript - XIS UI Composition / Custom JavaScript Custom JavaScript XIS tries to remove routine frontend plumbing, not the possibility of writing JavaScript. Extension files, custom EL functions, and global browser behavior remain available for cases where the UI really needs them. Custom JavaScript is an escape hatch, not the normal XIS programming model. Start with pages, frontlets, actions, forms, validation, refresh events, and template expressions. Add JavaScript only when the browser itself is the thing you need to control. Use JavaScript where it earns its place Good reasons include rich browser controls, canvas/WebGL scenes, custom keyboard or pointer handling, specialized visualization, or integration with a browser-only library. Ordinary form validation, navigation, and model updates should usually remain in XIS. A useful rule of thumb: if the code decides business state, permissions, validation, persistence, or navigation, keep it in Java. If it adapts a browser-only interaction to an existing XIS action, formats a value for display, or integrates a browser library, JavaScript can be the right tool. The detailed reference is in the Custom JavaScript guide . It covers extension registration, reusable JavaScript artifacts, custom EL functions, global browser behavior, submitting XIS forms from JavaScript, CSS/static assets, and the limits of the integration-test browser model. Extension files JavaScript extensions should be registered explicitly and packaged as part of the application or a reusable web artifact. This makes the dependency visible and keeps generated XIS resources separate from application-specific browser behavior. Do not include application JavaScript with ordinary

title: Events and refresh
text: Events And Refresh - XIS UI Composition / Events and refresh Events and refresh XIS refresh events are invalidation signals, not a second business API. The browser receives a key and reloads the affected page or frontlet through the normal XIS flow. Refresh, not data push The event should usually say what is stale, not carry the full replacement model. This keeps the model source on the server and prevents a second client-side synchronization protocol from growing beside the normal page and frontlet lifecycle. import one.xis.Action; import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; import one.xis.RefreshEventPublisher; import one.xis.RefreshOnUpdateEvents; @Page("/cart.html") @RefreshOnUpdateEvents("cart-updated") class CartPage { private final RefreshEventPublisher refreshEvents; CartPage(RefreshEventPublisher refreshEvents) { this.refreshEvents = refreshEvents; } @ModelData("cart") CartView cart() { return cartService.currentCart(); } @Action("addToCart") void addToCart(long productId) { cartService.add(productId); refreshEvents.publishToAll("cart-updated"); } } Targets Events can be broad or narrow. Some events are useful for all connected clients, some only for one authenticated user, and some only for one browser session. Choosing the narrowest useful target keeps the browser calm and avoids unnecessary refreshes. refreshEvents.publishToAll("cart-updated"); refreshEvents.publishToClient("cart-updated", clientId); refreshEvents.publishToUser("cart-updated", userId); refreshEvents.publishToAllUsers("account-notice-updated"); SSE transport Server-sent events are a good fit for XIS refresh signals because they are one-way, HTTP-friendly, and simpler to operate than a bidirectional socket when the application only needs server-to-browser notifications. The event channel should stay small. Business data is still loaded by the normal XIS request that follows the event. Consistency model A refresh event is allowed to be slightly late. It should not make the browser invent state. If the browser misses an event, the next explicit navigation, action, or refresh request must still reconstruct the correct state from the server.

title: Frontlets and theme
text:
Frontlets And Theme - XIS UI Composition / Frontlets and theme Frontlets and theme Frontlets, containers, includes, reusable artifacts, replaceable templates, and the optional XIS theme belong together: they are the composition layer before an application becomes distributed. Frontlets A frontlet should own a small, coherent part of the UI: navigation, a summary widget, a list fragment, a toolbar, or a reusable edit block. It has its own model data and can refresh without replacing the whole page. Includes and templates Includes are useful for shared markup that does not need its own controller model. Frontlets are better when the fragment has behavior, data, or refresh semantics. Keeping that distinction clear prevents small template helpers from turning into hidden application modules. Theme XIS should provide enough theme structure that applications start with a coherent look, but applications must be able to override styling. The theme is a default, not an ownership claim over every visual decision. Drag and drop Drag-and-drop interactions should still end in server actions. The browser can carry a small payload, but the server decides whether the move is allowed and what must refresh afterwards. This keeps the interaction testable in the same way as links and forms. Example: product page as team boundary A shop product page is a good example because everything revolves around the same product, but the data usually belongs to different systems and teams. Catalog data, current stock, delivery price, discounts, store pickup, and cart handling are not necessarily owned by the same service. The page can still look like one product page. XIS can compose it from specialized frontlets that all read the product id from the page URL with @PathVariable("id") . The product id is therefore not repeated as a frontlet parameter.

${product.name}

@Page("/products/{id}.html") class ProductPage { @ModelData("product") ProductHeader product(@PathVariable("id") long productId) { return catalogService.header(productId); } } @Frontlet class StockAndPickupFrontlet { @ModelData("stock") StockStatus stock(@PathVariable("id") long productId) { return inventoryService.status(productId); } } The catalog team can own the product description. The logistics team can own stock and pickup. The pricing team can own discounts and delivery price. The checkout team can own the add-to-cart workflow. This makes a frontlet an organizational pattern as much as a UI pattern: it is a possible team slice. This is optional. A XIS application can also be built from ordinary pages without frontlets and still behave like an SPA. Frontlets become valuable when a screen naturally contains independently owned or independently refreshed parts.

title: Server-driven SPA development for JVM teams
text:
XIS Documentation Documentation / Overview Server-driven SPA development for JVM teams XIS lets Java, Kotlin, and Groovy developers build interactive SPA-style applications from backend controllers and plain HTML templates. The programming model feels close to classic request/response development, while the browser is updated by the XIS JavaScript runtime without handwritten frontend application plumbing. Core idea. A feature should be understandable as a vertical slice: controller, template, tests, and optional persistence live close to each other. XIS supplies transport, synchronization, validation feedback, navigation, and partial refreshes. import one.xis.ModelData; import one.xis.Page; @Page("/hello.html") class HelloPage { @ModelData("message") String message() { return "Hello from XIS"; } }

${message}

Who XIS is for XIS is aimed at backend-oriented JVM teams that still want a modern browser experience. You should be able to build pages, forms, validation feedback, navigation, refreshes, and modal interactions without first creating a separate REST contract, DTO layer, frontend client, and frontend state model. That does not mean JavaScript is forbidden. It means JavaScript is no longer required for routine application plumbing. When a feature genuinely needs browser-side behavior, XIS offers extension points for custom JavaScript and custom expression-language functions. Start here Quickstart Create a small XIS app, run it, validate it, and add the first test without reading the full guide first. Tutorial A guided example application will grow chapter by chapter. Planned, not published yet. App wizard Create an application skeleton and download it as a ready-to-open project archive. Understand XIS How the runtime works Pages, model data, actions, DOM updates, and why XIS feels like request/response while behaving like an SPA. Pages and templates How backend controllers and plain HTML templates produce interactive browser pages. Frontlets and composition Split a page into focused UI fragments without introducing a separate frontend application model. Actions and navigation Call Java methods from links, buttons, forms, modals, and drag-and-drop without creating REST plumbing. Documentation Runtime overview Choose XIS Boot, XIS Spring, native images, or the advanced HTTP controller path intentionally. SQL and MongoDB XIS SQL uses repository operations. Loaded objects do not silently flush themselves into the database. Operations Build runnable jars or GraalVM native executables with generated XIS metadata. Complete guide Use the full static guide for every annotation, tag, task, persistence feature, security variant, and advanced topic. Index Find annotations, interfaces, response types, repository APIs, test helpers, and extension points alphabetically or by topic. Documentation goals The documentation starts with a small conceptual overview and then moves into practical, complete reference chapters. Quickstart and, later, tutorials have their own place because they are learning paths rather than reference material.
